content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What are some realistic “competence porn” movies?

**Score:** 63
**Body:** Think Mike Ehrmantrout (or a team of Mikes) just doing some job but for 2 hours straight

And it has to be realistic. People who actually work in that field should look at it and go “yep that looks about right”

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moneyball is probably one of the most popular for a while now.
2. Michael Mann's Thief.
3. I love these types of movies!

The Martian - every single character was competent otherwise the mission would have failed 

The Oceans movies - not super realistic but grounded

Drive - the Driver is a career bad guy who wanted to be good

Nightcrawler - Lou is not initially an expert at his vocation but he's exceedingly competent at being an amoral psychopath who is ambitious

Thomas Crown Affair - Pierce as an art thief who just does it for the love of the game

Heat - just an awesome movie
4. I think Apollo 13 is sort of the gold standard for this right?
5. High and Low (1963) by Akira Kurosawa. The second half of the movie is pure competence porn.
6. Black Dynamite
  - You can't more competent than "I threw that shit before I walked in the room"
7. A Man Escaped (1956) A step by step depiction of a man implementing his escape plan from a Nazi prison during WW2
8. [The Big Short](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/318846-the-big-short) (2015)
  - Margin Call is a great follow to the Big Short

## 2. Need some must watch 80s movies

**Score:** 41
**Body:** Just finished the back to the future trilogy and I’m looking for more 80s movies in that style. I mostly like mainstream stuff but I’m down to try some hidden movies as well. I’ve seen the back to the future movies as well as Jurassic park, Indiana jones, Star Wars, the goonies, Ferris bueller, and tron. There might be more but I can’t think of them right now.

Edit: wow I didn’t think I’d get so many responses, I’ve got a LOOOONG list of things to watch now. Thank you everyone!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. the outsiders 

stand by me

the lost boys 

the princess bride

gremlins
2. Just watched "Big Trouble in Little China" tonight. Its a little cheesy but creative and fun!
  - I mean that movie is legendary.
3. You’ve seen Ferris Bueller but have you seen the other John Hughes movies? Those are peak 80s. Breakfast Club, Sixteen Candles, Pretty in Pink, Weird Science. 
4. The Thing for an all-time 80s horror flick. Then Top Gun to level things out
5. Better Off Dead
6. Ghostbusters (1984)

Trading Places (1983)

Beverly Hills Cop (1984)?

Terminator (1984) ?

Clue (1985) ?

Lethal Weapon (1987)

Mad Max 2 + 3 ?

The Last Starfighter (1984) ?

Star Trek movies

Gremlins (1984) ?

Young Guns (1988) ?

The Burbs (1989)

Real Genius (1985) ?

National Lampoons Christmas Vacation (1989) ?

Uncle Buck (1989) ?

Fletch (1985) ?

Weekend at Bernies (1989) ?

Romancing the Stone (1984) and The Jewel of the Nile (1985)

Scrooged (1988)
7. Real Genius. Hilarious coming of age comedy
8. Mad Max: The Road Warrior
9. The Breakfast Club, Say Anything, Labyrinth

## 3. Movies that inspire kindness?

**Score:** 40
**Body:** So, I watched Amelie last night, and before that Lars and the Real Girl, and both just left me feeling so happy and hopeful. I love the way these movies remind you to be kind and a little more understanding. Do you know any other films with this same vibe? Sweet, gentle, heartwarming stories about kindness, empathy, and community.

Also, it doesn’t have to be, but bonus points if it’s family‑friendly, something I could throw on to watch with my family on Thanksgiving.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Litlle Miss Sunshine.
2. Pay it Forward (2000)
3. Spirited Away (2001)
4. Chocolat makes me feel like this.
5. Paddington & Paddington 2
6. Lady in the Water
7. Mrs. Harris goes to Paris
8. The Intern, with Anne Hathaway and Robert DeNiro, perhaps?
9. A Christmas Carol. one of the oldest xmas movies about a greedy rich guy
10. It’s A Wonderful Life

## 4. Movies that make you go wow!!!

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Hello Goodfellas. I am looking for movies for my ADHD brain, which keeps me hooked throughout and makes me go, "Wow, what a movie!" I still remember the first time I watched The Sixth Sense. I was screaming at my pillow screaming holy damn this is lit. Want to relieve that feeling. Thanks!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. uncut gems
2. Have you watched Shutter Island? Try it
  - If you want that mind blown feeling again watch Prisoners it grabs you fast and the ending hits hard you’ll be locked in the whole time
3. The Game (1997) if you haven't watched it yet. I was on the edge of my seat the entire movie!
4. Interstellar was more than a movie. It was an experience. 

I've been on this planet for 50 years and I've seen quite a few movies in my life but that one was extraordinary.  It really opened up my mind and made me really question a lot of things in our crazy universe.  I started reading a lot about space, physics, other planets - their geographical makeup and weather and their stars.  Humans and the planet we come from are a microscopic grain of sand in the massive ocean.


Interstellar really made me go wow!
  - I watched this on the big screen, I agree completely. This was indeed an experience!
5. This is going to be different for every person. What makes one person say "wow" may make another snore. That being said I would recommend based on your love for Goodfellas and The Sixth Sense:

Unbreakable, Split, Glass - M. Night Shyamalan Trilogy that is exciting and has a great cast.

Sleepers, The Godfather Trilogy, A Bronx Tale - Based on your enjoyment of Crime movies like goodfellas.
6. Memento
7. Natural Born Killers (1994)

Pulp Fiction (1994)

Dead Man (1995)

Irréversible (2002)

Crank (2006)

Enter the Void (2009)

Essential Killing (2010)

Good Time (2017)

Warfare (2025)
8. Parasite. One of the best movies of all time

## 5. Movies where the main character is a normal person but gets brought into an exciting adventure

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Title is pretty self-explanatory, preferably if the story has an element of fantasy

Examples I can think of off the top of my head

Matrix

Wanted

Doctor Strange

Harry Potter

Tv shows also welcome

Seems like a plot that would work well with an anime

Edit: think what I'm looking for is when the character has hidden powers or skills they were originally unaware of - like the examples given

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hitchhikers Guide to the Galaxy
2. North by Northwest
3. GALAXY QUEST!
4. Big Trouble in Little China
5. The Last Starfighter (1984)
6. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
7. Romancing the Stone
8. Dogma
9. Evil dead
10. After Hours

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name a famous actor that appeared in the movie but died in the first half

**Score:** 2976
**Body:** Liam Neeson in Gangs of New York.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sam l Jackson and dwayne Johnson in "The other guys"
  - "Aim for the bushes"
2. Drew Barrymore: Scream.
  - This is a good one to mention. Drew Barrymore was brought in to have a big name on the project, but at her suggestion, was killed off in the opening sequence. Some people thought this was a cheap bait and switch on the part of the producers, but Drew’s thinking was that if she was killed off, anybody in the movie was fair game.
3. Liam Neeson again in Kingdom of Heaven.
4. Janet Leigh in Psycho
5. Julianne Moore: Children of Men
6. Jim Carrey in Kick-Ass 2. One of the few things I remember about the film
7. Bryan Cranston Godzilla 2014
8. Ben Affleck in Smokin Aces.

## 2. Do you think Christy (2025) flopped at the box office because of Sydney Sweeney?

**Score:** 1523
**Body:** So I’ve been thinking about Christy (2025) and the conversation surrounding its box office performance. A lot of people online have been pointing fingers at Sydney Sweeney, saying her casting was the reason the movie underperformed financially.

If another actress had been cast in the lead role, do you think the film would’ve performed differently at the box office?

Would the outcome have changed with a different face attached? Or was the movie always heading toward a flop regardless of who starred?

Curious to hear what you all think especially from people who actually saw it.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It flopped because it's a biopic about a pretty unknown female boxer from the 90s.
  - This is 90% of it. The other 10% is that the kind of person who might see that movie probably doesn't care for sydney Sweeney. 

Too many ways to lose people. Biopic, personally I'm usually out on that. Sports movie, a lot of people now aren't going. Female boxer from the 90s, ok that's incredibly niche and you have entire swaths of the population that scoff at women's sports. Sydney sweeny, you didn't have many people left but you bummed some of them out just with that. 

Say you're a queer woman who loves sports and artsy movies, that's already a small market but this should be perfect for you. How many queer sportsy artsy women are mad at sydney Sweeney right now? Even if its 25% that's too big a piece of too small a pie.
    - I think this is the best summary of why it was never going to work that I've personally read. 
  - That and marketing, because i had not even heard that this movie existed until now, much less do i have any idea who the boxer is/was.
2. It was a mistake to put it in theaters; shoulda been a streaming release. 

No amount of marketing could overcome the fact the story/visual experience isn’t interesting enough to spend time and treasure on a night out. 

A lot of new movies fall into this trap.
3. I don't think this movie was ever going to make much no matter who was in it. Giving it a wide release was not a good idea, it just made it look like an even bigger flop than it was probably already going to be.
  - Which is too bad because the real Christy is a badass
4. This looks like a basic cable movie of the week from 2004.

No one knows or cares who this boxer is, so her biopic isn’t particularly a draw.

Also, most people over 30 don’t know/care about Sydney Sweeney,

Most women under 30 don’t like Sydney Sweeney because she’s perceived as rightwing/ 
oversexualized in a very conventional, old school way,

Most men under 30 don’t care about a movie where Sydney Sweeney is ugly/jacked,

And most cinephile types don’t take Sydney Sweeney seriously as an actress.

So who exactly is the audience for this?
  - Wow. Nailed it.
5. I used to be a fairly avid boxing fan back in her day and I remember her well.  Zero interest in paying a dime for this.

## 3. What is the most overrated movie of all time?

**Score:** 345

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shakespeare in Love beating out Saving Private Ryan for best picture, will never ever understand that
  - Harvey Weinstein strong arming the Academy with his trademark threats
    - Did he threaten to show them his testicle thigh?
2. I liked the twist ending in Titanic. I was not expecting the unsinkable ship to sink.
  - How do you think the unthinkable?

>!With an itheberg.!<
3. Not Titanic, that’s for sure
  - Man I wonder what reddit would have been like during Titanic’s run.
    - Hahah you think Oppenheimer was a shit show. This would’ve been back to the nth degree. Literally every single debate would’ve been about the stupid door.
      - Still is
4. These daily threads of the same question make me think Reddit just doesn't enjoy movies

## 4. Vanilla Sky Ending

**Score:** 170
**Body:** i've seen lots of sad movie scenes/endings, titanic, gladiator, saving private ryan - but this film just hits me different, I can't quite put my finger on it but I cry my eyes out as soon as "sigur ros" starts playing

I think it's the fact all of us always have that thought; "what if" what if my life tired out differently & we found total happiness & falling in love with someone 

plus maybe the look penelope cruz gives tom as he's just about to jump, when she kind of smiles at him with her eyes & gives him a wave but looks a bit defeated herself 

"i'll find you again... when we are both cats" 

movie magic 




### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The tone of that final scene is very special and dreamlike - "Elevator Beat" by Nancy Wilson does a lot of the lifting in the buildup to that final scene. I'm not sure the entire movie aged as well as some other Cameron Crowe movies, but I still think this one is underrated if only because of that final ten minutes, starting with Good Vibrations.
  - So a little story about this ending. When I was in 8th grade, I used to watch this movie on repeat in my basement. I was absolutely obsessed with the raw emotional depth of it, but also the soundtrack. I spent so much time trying to figure out some of those unknown names that weren't featured on the original OST. Freur's Doot Doot really resonated with me (also not on the soundtrack). But the most challenging one to find, which I had to go deep into the heart of the limited internet at that time, was a song called Njosnavelin (Untitled #4) by a then unknown Icelandic band named Sigur Ros. Apparently they were on the soundtrack the whole time, it featured another song of theirs and I didn't put two and two together. 

That record completely changed my musical taste and DNA. It's ingrained in my mind - alongside that scene. Something that will be a part of me forever.
2. This is one of the beautiful endings ever seen I've seen 2, 3 times for tht ending
3. “Every passing minute is another opportunity to turn it all around.”

Or something like that. 
Love that quote.
4. Hugely underrated movie. 

I’ll be honest: I’ve cried to it more than once.
5. Open your eyes (everything in its right place/ Radiohead song) scene in the start is pure magic
6. I watched this movie not knowing a thing. What a mind fuck.
  - Fun story: I watched this movie in theaters shortly after it came out. A few weeks later I fell asleep on my couch. Woke up in the middle of the night to *Abre los ojos* playing on tv. Same story. Still has Penelope Cruz. But in Spanish and came out 4 years earlier. I thought I was tripping balls.
    - Did you scream for tech support? I would've.
7. Great movie

## 5. "Bandits" is such a funny crime-comedy-drama movie that doesn't get near as much love as it deserves.

**Score:** 118
**Body:** Bruce Willis and Billy Bob Thornton were such a classic duo in this movie. And Cate Blanchett's performance was pretty damn good, I thought. There is something comforting about this movie that I love, I dont know what it is. Maybe its the Pacific-Northwest setting where it is always raining the movie (I love gloomy, rainy days), maybe its the back and forth bickering between Bruce Willis' and Billy Bob Thornton's characters, who have polar opposite personalities, making them the perfect bank robbing duo due to their different thought processes. I dont know. I just think it is a flat out entertaining movie. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bandits is one of those movies you throw on thinking it’ll be background noise and then suddenly you’re locked in like it’s a comfort blanket. Willis and Thornton had unreal chemistry, it’s wild this one just kinda slipped through the cracks.
2. Very underrated movie
3. This may have been the movie Cate Blanchett snuck in and stole my heart. By the time I saw The Aviator and Benjamin Button, it was clear that the damage was done, proving that it happened years prior. She is coquettish and charming as hell.
4. Beavers and ducks!
5. This and Pushing Tin
6. Always looking out for burning feathers
7. Holding out for a hero
  - Turn around bright eyes……
8. Okay, no more juice boxes, Chloe. And also Mark Knopfler's song Rudiger on the soundtrack.
9. Highly underrated movie!  I stumbled across this movie years ago when I was bored and surfing for a movie to watch.  And I loved it!  I started telling other people about it and no one had ever heard of it.  Still today, nobody remembers the movie.  But it is one I will watch each time I see it.  One of my favorites!

The dialog between Bruce Willis and Billy Bob Thornton is comedy genius.  One minute you think they hate each other, then you realize they are are best friends and have each other’s backs.  Such a great team and the way the movie pulls everything together in the finale is perfect.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Theaters are struggling largely due to the quality of Hollywood movies

**Score:** 121
**Body:** There are many factors that are contributing to the demise of movie theaters, but why do so many people choose to ignore one of the most obvious reasons?

The common reasons that are parroted include the society-wide adoption of streaming services, the in-theater behavior of audiences, the rising ticket and concession prices, the availability of home theater equipment etc..

Sure, these all contribute in some capacity to the decline of movie theaters, but what is conveniently ignored is how they are symptoms rather than the cause.

Hollywood doesn't make movies like they used to, and many people realize this. Why spend all this money gambling at your local theater when Hollywood's track record over the last 20 to 30 years has been so rotten. Nowadays, people have nearly the entire catalogue of films from every studio at their fingertips, which only constantly reinforces their skepticism about the latest theatrical releases. I don't place most of the blame on the theaters, it is Hollywood that is gutting them, especially when you consider how bottom of the barrel the competition is from streaming services.

PG Rated movies are selling out theaters this year. The younger demographic is clearly interested in seeing movies in theaters, despite not growning up in a time when theatergoing was a societal norm. So much for the excuses that young people would rather stay at home with a tablet or phone watching youtube, playing video games, or scrolling social media. Zootopia 2, Minecraft, Lilo & Stitch are dominating the box office.

If anything, it is older audiences who have increasingly checked out, but who can blame them? They know how much better Hollywood movies were decades ago, and they can just rewatch them at home.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you're talking about blockbusters sure, but in theaters showing artsier stuff there's plenty of great indie films coming out. I've been happily surprised with turnout in the theaters for movies like that along with older "classic" film screenings, though i do live in a major city with probably a bigger crowd interested in that sort of thing.
  - *but in theaters showing artsier stuff*

Unfortunately, you're describing an increasingly small number of places in the US. The nearest theater that still regularly shows art/foreign films is, no joke, 200 miles away from me.
  - Yeah I remember walking blind into Didi and it was one of my favs of 2024
2. We've always had bad and good movies, what changed was the big studios swallowing up all the smaller ones and being risk averse. Don't overlook the cost of going to the theater it is a big factor.
3. There’s no real data here beyond vague “vibes” to support your claim. Just hand-waving about “films these days.” Your point about kids’ films selling out is anecdotal at best, sure, opening weekends might do well, but there are plenty of near-empty theaters the rest of the time.

You’re trying to link a highly subjective idea of “film quality” (itself debated in the comments) to broad trends in theater attendance. That doesn’t hold up. The reality is far more complex: economic factors, ticket pricing, streaming, distribution changes, and consumer behavior all interact to drive these trends far more than the supposed decline in film quality.
4. Great movies flop all the time. People are only motivated to go out for a very small selection of films and those tend to not be very good. All kinds of slop makes up what still makes money. The box office isn't tied to quality.
5. Has hollywood's track record really been that bad since 1995? There are tons of great films that have come out between 1995 and the present. You may have a point for the last 5, mayyybe 10 years in certain genres, but that feels like its really stretching your argument quite a bit.
  - https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/s/zAF6Mx2Qrq

This will likely just be a rant he spams  weekly
6. It's also a bit of a self-fulfilling prophecy. Studios act like the only films that bring audiences into cinemas are superhero or nostalgia-driven movies, so they only promote those types of movies. That means that these films become increasingly risk-averse because their budgets are so bloated, which eventually just leads to audiences getting bored and avoiding them. Meanwhile, good movies don't get marketed enough and fail at the box office, which just "proves" to the studios that audiences don't care about them.

I agree with you, there's a real hunger for experiences that aren't tied to our phones and TVs, but studio execs are trapped in this self-defeating mentality where they just assume everybody's happier sitting at home than going to the cinema. If you don't actually believe in your product, why would anybody else?
7. There have been terrible movies made in every era since the invention of movies. We simply often forget the bad. The idea that movies have gotten worse in more recent times is always such a silly take. And to be frank, a movies box office should not determine whether it’s considered good or bad. I’ve seen movies I thought were awful make a billion dollars and movies I thought were great make nothing.

## 2. I actually liked GDT's Frankenstein

**Score:** 74
**Body:** I've seen a few words of criticism levied at the new adaptation of Frankenstein. I read the book years ago and thought it was a fantastic piece of gothic literature. I've seen posts talk about how the film doesn't adopt the book's complex characters and waters down what makes the original story interesting. My main issue with these remarks is that so much of the movie's story is different from the original book that I would falter to even call this an adaptation. Rather I felt this film was a modernization of the story told through the lens of a different theme. 

In the book, Victor's curiosity which drives him to create the monster is born out of a childhood fascination with outdated alchemists. When these studies fail to deliver him academic merit, he turns and masters the modern sciences. It's his scientific fluency and his innate curiosity of the natural world that drives him to create a living creature from the dead tissue of corpses. But at the instance of the creature's creation, he abandons it in horror. The book is meant to tell us that the inhumane and ruthless pursuit of knowledge will be our undoing. That pursuit exists in Frankenstein as well as the sea captain Walton who's quest for the North Pole is done at the expense of his crew. 

The movie however has a completely different version of Victor's backstory. Victor suffers the lose of his mother, who's death he blames on his cold and ruthless father. A father that forces the study of anatomy onto Victor and beats his face when he isn't able to demonstrate proper intelligence. This is a deliberate choice. The movie is painting Victor as someone who's ambitions are born from spite of his father. His relationship with his father is much more central to his goals than in the book. In the movie Victor even refers to his name as something his father gave him. 

The movie also changes the instance when Victor abandons his creation. Instead film-Victor takes time to raise the creature as his own, like a father would his own son. But what begins as a brief moment of joy quickly becomes a vicious cycle of abuse. Like Victor's father before him, Victor is obsessed with beating proper intelligence out of his creation. 

The book's interpretation of the importance of family is quite black and white. The Frankensteins are an idealized group of people. Seclusion and isolation, the opposite of a family, are the cause of the misfortune that falls on story's cast of characters. Victor's seclusion from his loved ones and his secrecy allows him to go too far with his creation. Similarly seclusion causes for the misery that befalls the creature. 

Yet the film's characters never really deal with seclusion. Victor is constantly undergoing his experiments in the company of his benefactor, his brother, and at times even his would be tryst Elizabeth. It's Frankenstein's heartless treatment of his creation that is the true cause of misfortune. Family is not that answer in the film like it is in the book. In fact sometimes it can be quite the opposite. It's a thesis that runs quite contrary to the novel. 

Of course the film isn't perfect. It largely takes from a story that wasn't written to be about the relationships between fathers and their sons. It does have it's moments though. I was quite touched when Victor referred to his creation as his "son" at the very end. Though I can imagine some audiences weren't to keen on the film's lack of subtly. 

  
But that's just what I thought coming from a person who liked the movie and hasn't read the book in a while. Feel free to leave your thoughts, whether you agree or not. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What is up with these comments, people aren’t even reading the post and just trying to take over the thread with their negative opinions, that’s not how discussions work, people!

And yeah OP I agree, as someone who loved the book and enjoyed the adaptation, there’s a point where the viewer has to accept that they’re both going to be different interpretations of the same story. They’re both going for very different things, and it’s a bit lazy to say something is bad because it’s not the same.

Obviously you can’t separate the film from the book, but we can’t expect every adaptation to be a 1:1. I do like the contrast you pointed out about film focusing on the interpersonal relationships, while the book focused more on external relationships.
  - Honestly, this is why I got sick of discussing film with a lot of film buffs: they would step over each other to point out all the things they didn't like about a movie. You could watch a perfect movie but they would want to focus on that tiny imperfection that they caught. I soon realized that I was watching movies looking for what I could find wrong with them.

My wife and I are in our forties with a toddler, a home to maintain, jobs, side hustles, aging parents, etc. We rarely find time to watch movies. So when I do I just want to enjoy myself, I want to be taken to another place and indulge in a craft that a lot of people worked hard on.
2. I kinda liked it too.  I've read the book and agree with much of what OP states - but at this point, Frankenstein is a genre.  Go do what you can with it and have fun making a movie!
3. Two things I found interesting: There is a sliding scale of self-control and ambition as the three "generations" of Victor's father, Victor, and the monster follow one another. The ambitions of the previous generation and their "civilized" violence lead to problems with controlling violence in the subsequent generations.

Secondly, this is not just a myth, but Del Toro turns it into a myth about myth-making and the construction of narratives. We don't get to see Victor's father's point of view in any detail, but each one has a story from their perspective that may or may not be reliable but feels like truth to them, about what life has done to them, what they can aspire to, and what they have accomplished or are guilty of. The stylized and "artificial" elements some object to in the film are not entirely coincidental here either.

I wonder whether some of the elements some found inconsistent in the film and were "baffling" and "ineffective" choices shouldn't be seen instead as forms of nuance in this process of mythmaking, nuance that they fail to appreciate because they try to frame the film's concerns in terms of the book's concerns. As long as one only looks at the overt story and one doesn't read between the lines using the visual symbols and key dialogue lines, not to mention the general style, and one thinks of the emotional impact only in terms of the face value of a description of events, one isn't going to find this film very nuanced or as good as the book. To be clear, I haven't seen it a second time or absorbed everything I could to make this argument. But it seems to me there may just be more to the film even thematically than people are giving it credit for.
4. I liked the movie overall. I hated how the monster is his entire life's work and then he all of a sudden abhors it for no reason. I hated how the one woman in the entire movie basically does nothing to advance the plot. Also, absolutely hated how the guy stubbornly gets chased halfway across the country just for there to be a halfassed the deathbed atonement in the last three minutes of the movie.
  - He abhors it because it isn’t perfect and doesn’t earn him validation from Elizabeth. In fact, Elizabeth prefers the creature to Victor, even referring to it as pure and innocent. 

The movie does a good enough job of showing Victor’s lust and infatuation with Elizabeth. It also foreshadows his treatment of his creation in every scene with his father.

Also foreshadowed is the ending, which wasn’t an after thought at all. One of the first lessons the old man teaches the creature is that forgiving someone who has wronged you is one of the greatest acts a human can do.

It’s one thing to not like the story you are watching, but you must have entered some kind of demented fugue state if you don’t remember the blatant set ups for those pivotal scenes. 
    - This.  Its an act of creation ("playing God") but without God's perfect love for His creation.  That's the whole point of the movie
  - For no reason?  Media literacy is in the trash.
  - >just for there to be a halfassed the deathbed atonement in the last three minutes of the movie.

That's how therapy works. You face your father, talk through your childhood trauma and forgive each other. The monster went through a therapy session with the old guy in the house who told him to forgive his father (this is literally advice they give you in therapy for abusive family relations). Then to let go of his generational trauma, he goes and actually discusses it in great detail with his father, who also explains his own passed down trauma. It's not a half-assed last minute atonement, it's literally the premise of the entire movie. They don't forgive each other for nothing, they both understand each other's situations because they talked through it for an entire night.
5. I swear, people are spoilt. It's great. It was clearly a real passion project for him; he had wanted to make it his whole life. It looks beautiful, it's watchable as hell, and the performances are excellent. It's probably his best film since Pan's Labyrinth. I don't know where all these experts on classic literature analysis came from all of a sudden (book tok?), but I think it's important to remember adaptations of beloved books always get this reaction, no matter how good they are, because some fans of the book will always feel they understand it best.

## 3. The Minority Tropes of One Battle After Another, Sanskrit Texts and the Roman Empire.

**Score:** 30
**Body:** A significant amount of criticism has been levied at One Battle After Another that the minority characters are presented as stereotypes.

Such criticism is noting something that is evidently true: At least five central minority characters in One Battle After Another are based on prominent minority tropes and stereotypes. That is evident from the range of the tropes, the naming of the characters, the casting of actors, and a reference to historical dramatic tropes.  

What the criticism may be missing is that those characters are intentionally presented as tropes, and that the use of stereotypes, tropes and dramatic conventions seem to be part of the story the film is trying to tell on a subtextual level. 

There are three common dramatic stereotypes of black women in modern American fiction: the Jezebel, the Sapphire, and the Mammy.

Perfidia embodies two of those “negative” stereotypes: the “Sapphire” or “angry black woman” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angry_black_woman) and the “Jezebel” or hypersexualised, predatory and immoral black woman (https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/BlackJezebelStereotype). Another character, Junglepussy, also embodies both of those stereotypes, and is a counterweight to Perfidia in going back and forth between the two (their discussion around “guns are for fun, pussy is for war” and the juxtaposition of Junglepussy’s sexual, rage-filled speech with the violence of the shooting of the guard show that anger/sexualization are two sides of the same stereotypes of black women.)

Deandra is the more “positive” stereotype, the “Mammy” or “strong black woman”. The Mammy archetype is a black woman (often slave or servant) that cares for her white master’s children. As the specific slave implications of the Mammy have become culturally unacceptable, the Mammy has shifted to the “strong black woman.” The strong black woman is “expected to display strength and independence by holding back their emotions and taking responsibility for other people's problems.” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strong_black_woman). Deandra, though a participant in the bank heist and logically a target of Lockjaw, foregoes protecting herself and turns instead to protecting and caring for Baby Charlene, and 16 years later picks up the same thread by immediately rushing to protect Willa despite the significant risk to herself. She only displays her emotions once the implications of her choice to dedicate herself to Willa become clear (she is likely going to be killed).

Sensei Sergio is the “Magical Minority” (https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/MagicalMinorityPerson) which is related to the “Magical Negro” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magical_Negro). The Magical Minority uses mystical powers and folk wisdom to keep the white main character that they support on the right path, both in practicality and ethically, and often sacrifices themselves for the main character’s arc. I’ve written previously about Sensei and this trope: https://www.reddit.com/r/paulthomasanderson/comments/1o8g80t/the_magical_latino_ptas_subtext_for_sensei_in_one/ .

Avanti Q is the “Noble Savage” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noble_savage). The noble savage is a member of an uncivilized group (such as Indians and bounty hunters) that is uncorrupted by civilized society and is in harmony with nature. The noble savage often sacrifices himself to save the civilized or white characters under his protection out of a sense of duty to their moral code. Avanti being unwilling to take Lockjaw’s money to kill Willa shows he is uncorrupted by the “civilized” people who surround him, the CAC and MKU. Moreover, his reason for eventually sacrificing himself - “I don’t do kids” - is in harmony with the natural order to protect kids inherent in our paternal and maternal instincts. The actor who portrays Avanti, Eric Schweig, played the character Uncas in The Last of the Mohicans, a character that in both the novel and the film is a proto-archetype of the noble savage, and who sacrificed himself to save the heroine. PTA has noted in an interview that this was related to why Schweig was cast in OBAA.

It is notable that these minority characters all sacrifice themselves in line with their tropes. Perfidia sacrifices herself for her selfish and immoral choices, driven by both her anger and uncontrolled desires. Deandra and Avanti sacrifice themselves out of their sense of duty towards their protectee, Willa. And Sensei sacrifices his freedom for Bob’s character arc.

The characters’ relationships to their tropes are revealed in their names:

Perfidia means “betrayal” in Spanish and Italian, from the same Latin root as perfidy. Perfidia betrays based on her two stereotypical aspects. She sexually betrays both Pat and Lockjaw (in line with her Jezebel trope, by cheating on Pat with Lockjaw, and by telling Lockjaw she loved him but later that her pussy doesn’t pop for him). And she betrays Pat, Willa and her revolution through her actions born out of her anger (in line with the Sapphire trope, her anger at Pat and his love for Charlene, motherhood generally, society generally, and whatever trauma she is shown as running from, leads to her shooting the guard, betraying her co-conspirators, and abandoning Pat and Baby Charlene). Meanwhile, her counterpart “Junglepussy” has a name that is in keeping with the Jezebel archetype, and that is the actual performer name of the actor playing her role, underscoring the role casting and naming play in these trope characters.

Deandra is a name that combines the Welsh Dee (“Dark” or “Black”) with the Greek Andrea (“Strong”).

Sergio is derived from the Latin for “servant” or “guardian.” And Sensei is a title connoting a mystical, guiding minority. (I discuss St. Carlos later in this post.)

Avanti is a name that comes from Sanskrit meaning “protector.” I have not figured out why he has a “Q” in his name, but I am certain there is a reason.

Avanti’s name also contains a deep clue to his existence as a trope. There is an ancient Sanskrit text called the *Natyashastra*, which is a treatise on drama and has topics that include dramatic composition, the structure of plays, and dramatic conventions, i.e., the tropes that ancient Hindu drama would follow (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natya_Shastra)

In this text can be found Chapter XVIII - Rules on the use of Languages (you can read a translation here: https://www.wisdomlib.org/hinduism/book/the-natyashastra/d/doc210088.html ). This chapter sets out how different types of characters would use different types of languages and dialects… i.e., the linguistic tropes the characters would follow. For example, in American drama a gangster would use an immigrant or Southie dialect, a surfer would use a Southern California dialect, while an educated ivy-leaguer would use a Brahmin dialect. Pun intended.

For these ~2,000 year old Sanskrit dramatic rules, the book lays out, for example, that “Prācyā” is the dialect spoken by Jesters, “Śaurasenī” is the dialect spoken by Heroines and their female friends, and “Dākṣiṇātyā” is the dialect of soldiers.

According to the *Natyashastra*, which characters are to speak the *”Āvantī”* dialect?

The “gallant crooks.” 

This ancient Hindu trope seems akin to the modern trope of the “noble crook.” Robin Hood is an archetypal example of the noble crook. The noble crook is much like the noble savage, in that as crooks/savages they live outside the codes of civilization, but nonetheless follow an internal code that is in harmony with a natural order/morality. In that sense, the difference between the two tropes is often only race.

Avanti exhibits the characteristics of both of the tropes, both as an Indian and as a bounty hunter. 

The *Natyashastra* text tells us that the speaker of the Avanti dialect, as a gallant crook, should be smooth, carrying charm with danger. He should smile slightly while speaking and use half-glances and pauses to imply calculation. The audience should both like him and distrust him. (https://natya-shastra.in/how-to-use-major-and-minor-dialects-in-performance/)

(I had originally thought Avanti’s name was a reference to the film *Avanti!* directed by one of PTA’s heroes, Billy Wilder. But as I am learning as I dig deeper into this ridiculously layered film, PTA often uses film homage as a near-surface interpretation that obscures the deeper significance behind many choices.)

Why did PTA write these minority characters as tropes? I’m not sure I fully understand just yet. But I think one clue lies in how Spike Lee defined the Magical Negro. Lee coined that term in 2001, in a series of lectures on college campuses. In these, he regularly pointed to the black characters in The Green Mile and The Legend of Bagger Vance. Speaking of Bagger Vance and the context of the time and place where the film takes place (Georgia in 1931), Lee said:

“Blacks are getting lynched left and right, and [Bagger Vance is] more concerned about improving Matt Damon's golf swing! ... I gotta sit down; I get mad just thinking about it. They're still doing the same old thing ... recycling the noble savage and the happy slave.”

PTA here does the opposite of that: he grounds Sensei in reality by showing him in his Latino Harriet Tubman operation. In essence, we are shown the real-world stakes for Sensei, before he inexplicably takes off to become a trope for the sake of Bob’s character arc. (Notably, it seems the Latino Harriet Tubman operation scenes were reportedly an idea from Benicio del Toro.)

If the minority characters are all tropes, what about the white characters?

The Christmas Adventurers are highly stereotyped. From their clothing choices (Izod and Patagonia) to their culinary tastes (for banana pancakes and chicken nuggets) to their language choices (bland corporate speak), the CAC are presented as absurdly stereotypical versions of white people, specifically the types that might be white supremacists.

Most of the CAC seem to have generic, bland and stereotypically Eurocentric names like Tim Smith, Bill Desmond, and Sandy Irvine. 

An exception is the leader of the CAC, Virgil Throckmorton. 

Virgil was the foundational poet of the Roman Empire. His epic poem The Aeneid tells the story of the founding and rise of Rome. Notably, Virgil created a certain mythology about Rome that led to their belief in their own supremacy. By creating a link between Rome and the fall of Troy through Aeneas, he legitimized the Roman Empire as the “true” historical empire destined to rule the earth by linking it to the Greeks, thereby mythologizing the supremacy of the Romans.

A macro view: Virgil is the *storyteller* who wove the myth of Rome that justified its rise, which leads to Europe and Western civilization’s expansion and dominance, which leads to the myth of white superiority in modern times. In that sense Virgil’s myth is the foundational myth of white supremacy. 

As for Throckmorton: Sir Francis Throckmorton was a key figure in the Counter-Reformation and architect of the “Throckmorton Plot”, an effort inside England to dethrone the Protestant Queen Elizabeth I and replace her with Mary, Queen of Scots, a Catholic, by causing a war between England and Spain. In other words, Throckmorton tried to restore the power of Rome in England by exerting control over the English throne.

Notably, Sergio St. Carlos’ last name is a reference to Saint Charles Borromeo, an Italian Archbishop that was also a key figure of the Counter-Reformation. In contrast to Throckmorton, St. Charles efforts at counter-reformation meant reforming Catholic doctrine to make it more appealing to Europeans in the throws of the Reformation.

In other words: both Throckmorton and St. Charles were active in the effort to defend Rome’s power in the face of the Reformation. Throckmorton tried to protect Rome’s power through violence and control. Saint Charles tried to protect Rome by softening the doctrine, and positioning the church closer to the people.

Both Throckmorton and St. Charles died in 1584. 

Where is all this going? Only PTA knows for sure. 

What seems clear to me is that all of the central characters of OBAA are racial stereotypes or tropes, EXCEPT for the two fathers and their daughter. Also, clear that the names of the characters are clues to who they are and to some role they play in both the surface level story as well as some subtextual story.

So what about Bob and Lockjaw? These two characters are essentially contrasting versions of a father, a stand in for PTA himself. There are some naming clues: Pat/PTA and Ferguson/Anderson. As for Lockjaw, the only clue I have is that a character in Magnolia, Claudia, references TMJ (a cause of lockjaw) and shows how it causes her jaw to click and lock up. Does lockjaw have a deeper meaning for PTA (perhaps an inside joke/reference to a different aspect of his personality)?

PTA nonetheless likely sees different aspects of his personality in both: the laid back, drug-using zen loser, and the competent, efficient, workaholic, award winner. 

The characters often rely on tropes about the types of fathers they are. That is no more on display than in the final scene, as we watch Lockjaw get carted away after killing himself to get the corner office (Suite 55, PTA’s current age), and Bob’s voiceover in contrast, saying he always wanted to be the “cool dad.” PTA’s views of which dad is preferable are fairly on the nose.

And Willa, of course, does not appear to be a trope or a stereotype. Young and innocent, she has yet to find her trope. (Willa’s name is Germanic for “resolute protector.”)

What does all this use of tropes and stereotypes mean? What are these deep, deep Easter eggs? haven’t arrived at a final answer yet. I think there is a lot of deeper meaning imbued that hasn’t quite been discovered yet, as viewers are pulled in different directions seeing what they want to see in this film, mostly related to their political views.

But I suspect part of the story is that these modern minority tropes all stem from ancient historical myths, such as the noble savage trope and the Madonna/whore complex. An overarching theme of the film is that history is cyclical and a never ending story of one battle after another - time doesn’t exist yet it controls us. Perfidia says nothing changed in 16 years, Sensei says the struggle has been going on for hundreds of years. The use of tropes that date back millennia indicates the story has been going on for longer than that.

Further, in addition to the use of tropes, the film regularly alludes to its use of filmmaker conventions. What might be considered homage is also showing how storytellers have been using conventions and tropes to tell stories over time, and how those stories are the myths that inform our current understanding of our world.

There is so much more to the film than this; this is not an attempt to “explain” the film through this lens. It is simply an observation that the use of tropes is inherently there, and is intentional, and that the naming of characters perhaps runs deeper than we understand.

What do you think?

1. Thanks for the rabbit hole, it was fun to dive down it. Not sure I believe that Bob isn't a stereotype (the disaffected druggie around which life whirls), and I think many of the others pull in that direction either to provide some levity or simply because we're dealing with film, which has to heavily elide time one way or another.

As for the names, I love the directions you went in and want to investigate more--given the source material is Pynchon and he marries ridiculous-sounding names with deep history cuts (I'm currently reading Shadow Ticket and it's a ball), I expected there to be as many insights as red herrings.
  - Thanks for the comment. I tried to point out that there are some stereotypes about Bob, particularly around the type of dad he is, but they are not *racial* stereotypes. Everyone but the three central characters appear to be some sort of racial stereotype.

“Stereotypes” is basically another way of saying storytelling shorthand, whether you call them tropes or dramatic conventions or whatever, even homage. And PTA seems to use a lot of them in this film, and the use of them seems to be part of the story.

An example: in the final “car chase” sequence, PTA appears to be deliberately creating ambiguity around whether Tim Smith is actually chasing Willa. There are no textual references to Tim being after Willa (the “make it clean” reference is ambiguous as to whether it includes Willa), and there are no obvious signs he knows who she is or is chasing her, and he exhibits no aggression towards her (she shoots him in cold blood, after which he reaches for his sidearm and she shoots him again, very similar to her mother 16 years before). Moreover, Willa has no idea who he is… as far as she is concerned he may just be a motorist driving a sports car through the desert.

In other words, PTA very deliberately set up a series of scenes to leave the audience *implying* that Tim is chasing her, but without ever making that explicit.

How did he do that? One way was by using the conventions of a car chase, and an homage. Throughout the movie we have been introduced to these two beautiful, powerful sports cars, and shown how fast they move. When the blue Mustang appears in the rearview mirror of the white Charger, the audience is cued… this is an important moment, these two cars are about to face off. And through the fact that the cars are a seeming homage to Bullitt (which also had a Mustang and a Charger in its famous car chase), PTA is both hinting at and obscuring that the car chase conventions established in films like Bullitt are what allow him to now show a car chase that the audience believes is a car chase, without ever actually establishing that a car chase is happening. 

A clue may also be that the cars are reversed. In Bullitt the Charger chases the Mustang. If PTA was just setting up a reverential homage, wouldn’t he have switched the cars so that Tim drove a Charger and Avanti a Mustang? I think PTA is implying this supposed homage is more than an homage… it’s potentially a way of deceiving the audience. 

As for the names, there are still a lot of names to explore. Howard Somerville, Laredo, Mae West.. . What do they mean? As for Talleyrand, he was a  Napoleonic diplomat. Per his Wikipedia page, historians perceive him in two ways: one as a skilled diplomat in support of Napoleon, and others as a traitor to the Napoleonic revolution. We only really see Talleyrand in two scenes: in the current day he seemingly exists only to tell a racial dirty joke as the call center supervisor. But in the prologue, he is shown observing and walking away as Laredo is taken down by Lockjaw. Is PTA suggesting Talleyrand may have been an informant? It’s unclear. Some things suggest he is not, in the current day at least.

1. It doesn’t feel like a true comeback so much as a recalibration of how the industry decides to frame someone again. An actor gets seen in a new light, lands a role with some visibility, and the press shapes a narrative around it. My sense is that bringing someone back requires an interesting enough personality, story, or style – and media willing to amplify it until it resonates with audiences again.
